stem: A connected graph has six vertices with degrees \( [[d1]], [[d2]], [[d3]], [[d4]], [[d5]], [[d6]] \). By the even-degree criterion, the existence of an Euler circuit or Euler path is determined by the number of odd-degree vertices (an Euler circuit needs 0, an Euler path needs exactly 2, otherwise neither exists). How many of the six vertices have odd degree?
solution: Checking each degree's parity, the odd-degree vertices among \( [[d1]], [[d2]], [[d3]], [[d4]], [[d5]], [[d6]] \) number [[answer]] (0 means an Euler circuit exists; 2 means only an Euler path exists; any other even count exceeding 2 means neither exists).
distractor_rationales: This counts the EVEN-degree vertices instead of the odd-degree ones.
This only checks the first three degrees instead of all six.
This overshoots the correct odd-degree count by 1.
This overshoots the correct odd-degree count by 2.
This undershoots the correct odd-degree count by 2.
This guesses the exactly-Euler-path count without actually checking the degrees.

stem: In the complete graph \( K_{[[n]]} \), every vertex has degree \( [[n]]-1 \). How many of the [[n]] vertices have ODD degree?
solution: Every vertex has degree \( [[n]]-1 \); this is odd exactly when \( [[n]] \) is even. So the count of odd-degree vertices is either \( [[n]] \) (if all degrees are odd) or \( 0 \) (if all degrees are even), giving \( [[n]]\cdot([[n]]-1 \bmod 2) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the shared degree \([[n]]-1\) itself, not the count of vertices carrying that degree.
This is one more than the shared degree \([[n]]-1\), not the count of odd-degree vertices.
This treats only half the vertices as having odd degree instead of checking the actual parity.
This double-counts the vertices instead of checking the actual parity of the shared degree.
This guesses a fixed small value unrelated to the actual parity check.
This guesses another fixed small value unrelated to the actual parity check.

stem: In the complete bipartite graph \( K_{[[m]],[[n]]} \), each of the [[m]] vertices on one side has degree [[n]], and each of the [[n]] vertices on the other side has degree [[m]]. How many of the \( [[m]]+[[n]] \) vertices have ODD degree?
solution: The [[m]] vertices on the first side have degree [[n]], contributing [[m]] odd-degree vertices when [[n]] is odd (else 0); symmetrically the [[n]] vertices on the other side contribute [[n]] odd-degree vertices when [[m]] is odd (else 0). Adding these gives \( [[m]]\cdot([[n]]\bmod 2) + [[n]]\cdot([[m]]\bmod 2) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This assumes every vertex on both sides is automatically odd-degree, regardless of actual parities.
This swaps which side's degree gets checked against which side's parity.
This reports only whether \(m\) and \(n\) are individually odd, not how many vertices that parity affects.
This only accounts for one side of the bipartition and ignores the other entirely.
This overshoots the correct odd-degree count by 1.
This assumes every vertex always has even degree regardless of the actual parities of \(m\) and \(n\).
